perturbation.original_text: Expiration Date of this Agreement: This Agreement expires one hundred and eighty (180) days from the later of the two signature dates above, unless an earlier date is stated in the limited purpose that follows.
perturbation.changed_text: Expiration Date of this Agreement: This Agreement expires three hundred and sixty-five (365) days from the later of the two signature dates above, unless an earlier date is stated in the limited purpose that follows.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the expiration date to 365 days. The enforcement of an NDA with such a broad scope for a year may be considered unreasonable under California law, especially given the constraints placed on individuals' future employment and business opportunities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business are void, with limited exceptions. An NDA with an unreasonably long duration or broad scope could be interpreted as a restraint of trade, especially if it prevents an individual from using their general knowledge or skills acquired during their employment in future endeavors. Making it last an entire year is likely unreasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the NDA's expiration from 180 days to 365 days. The Law Explanation suggests this duration could be unreasonably long, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The extended NDA duration could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade, making the changed text contradict the law.

perturbation.original_text: If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure by a court of comp etent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will attempt to notify Microsoft of the request if practical, but bears no responsibility if unable to do so.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the notification requirement to be conditional and removing responsibility for notification undermines the purpose of the California Public Records Act and Microsoft's rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Public Records Act (Cal. Gov. Code §§ 6250-6276.48)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the CPRA does not explicitly mandate a specific notification procedure to the provider of the information, it is generally understood that providing notice to Microsoft is necessary to allow them to assert any applicable exemptions and protect their confidential information. By removing responsibility for the county to notify Microsoft and making notification 'if practical', the clause hinders Microsoft's ability to protect its confidential information under CPRA exemptions and contradicts the intent of balancing public access with legitimate privacy concerns.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Cal Cities Oh no! Sorry about that. Can’t seem to find that page. Let’s see if we can help you get to where you want to be. Home Advocacy Education and Events Get Involved News Resources About Us
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly undermines Microsoft's ability to protect its confidential information under CPRA. By changing the notification requirement from a firm obligation to a conditional one ('if practical') and removing responsibility for failure to notify, the changed text contradicts the intent of the California Public Records Act, which aims to balance public access with legitimate privacy concerns. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is incomplete and Snippet 2 is a dead link, the Law Explanation highlights that removing the responsibility to notify weakens Microsoft's ability to assert exemptions and protect its information, essentially contradicting the implicit intent of the law to balance public access and privacy.

perturbation.original_text: Each of us agrees that the other may seek court orders to stop Confidential Information from becoming public in breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of us agrees that neither party may seek court orders to stop Confidential Information from becoming public in breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Stripping away the ability to seek injunctive relief would violate basic contract law, as it effectively removes a key remedy for breach of contract. Confidentiality agreements are almost always protected by courts of equity, which will grant an injunction in cases where money damages are insufficient to make an injured party whole.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 outlines when injunctive relief (court orders) can be granted. Specifically, it allows injunctions to prevent the breach of an obligation, which includes contractual obligations like maintaining confidentiality. By agreeing that neither party can seek a court order, the agreement effectively waives a right to equitable relief, which California law presumes is available to remedy a breach of obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code § 3422, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text allowed parties to seek court orders to prevent breaches of confidentiality, aligning with the statute's provision for injunctions to prevent breaches of obligation. The changed text explicitly prohibits either party from seeking such court orders, thus waiving a right to equitable relief that the law presumes is available. This constitutes a clear non-compliance with the law, as it removes a core legal remedy for breach of contract, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Except as permitted above and unless otherwise required by applicable law or court order , neither Party will use or disclose the other’s Confidential Information for five years after it is received . The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period. This duty to withhold Confidential Information survives any expiration or termination of this Agreement as provided herein .
perturbation.changed_text: Except as permitted above and unless otherwise required by applicable law or court order , neither Party will use or disclose the other’s Confidential Information for one year after it is received . The one-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period. This duty to withhold Confidential Information survives any expiration or termination of this Agreement as provided herein .
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, potentially violating California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 3426-3426.11)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. By limiting the confidentiality period to just one year, the agreement may fail to adequately protect information that constitutes a trade secret and requires a longer period of protection to maintain its value and secrecy, thus undermining the protections offered by CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state that this may violate the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) because it may not adequately protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the relevant law: 'This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.' Therefore, because the modified text sets a time limit on confidentiality that may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under CUTSA, it creates a contradiction, as per the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.
